item.title: Emotionally Specific Backchanneling
item.description:
Overview

Backchannels are the small verbal and nonverbal signals that communicate attention, understanding, and emotional engagement during conversation. This project examined how emotionally specific backchannels differ between human-human interaction and social human-robot interaction.

Research Focus

Rather than assuming that models trained on human conversation can be transferred directly to robots, the study compared interaction behavior across emotional contexts, including happy and sad exchanges. The goal was to identify where robot behavior needs its own interaction model instead of a direct copy of human data.

Contribution

The findings showed meaningful differences in emotionally specific backchanneling between human-human and human-robot settings. Those differences provide design evidence for interaction models that are better calibrated to a robot’s social role, helping make feedback more context-aware, intelligible, and engaging.

Project Links

item.title: Robot-Delivered Fall Prevention
item.description:
Overview

This project investigated whether a social robot can make a structured fall-prevention exercise program more accessible for community-dwelling older adults. The intervention adapted the Otago Exercise Program and walking activities into robot-led sessions intended to support repeated, unsupervised practice outside traditional clinician-led delivery.

Research Focus

The pilot examined feasibility, adherence, acceptance of robot-delivered exercise, and early trends in physical and self-reported outcomes. Participants completed robot-led exercise sessions three times per week for four weeks while the study collected measures of balance confidence, falls self-efficacy, physical performance, and attitudes toward the technology.

Contribution

The study found that participants completed between 7 and 12 sessions, with positive attitudes toward technology and perceived enjoyment remaining favorable. The results also showed promising trends in balance confidence and physical performance while identifying technical constraints that matter when moving robot-supported exercise into everyday settings.

Project Links

item.title: Robot-Mediated Group Instruction
item.description:
Overview

This project designed and studied a robot-mediated group-instruction intervention for children with autism spectrum disorder. The work addresses a practical limitation in much of the existing literature: social-robot interventions are often evaluated one-on-one, while classroom participation requires children to navigate shared attention, peers, and changing group dynamics.

Research Focus

The intervention gave children opportunities to practice attending to and responding to instructional requests in a group setting. The study compared learning behavior during robot-led and human-led sessions across multiple intervention sessions, with an emphasis on whether the robot could support sustained participation rather than only a short novelty effect.

Contribution

Learning behaviors were similar across robot-led and human-led instruction, and the study did not observe a novelty effect over repeated sessions. The project contributes evidence that social robots can be designed as structured educational partners for group-interaction practice, not only as one-to-one tutoring systems.

Project Links

item.title: NAO Human Motion Imitation
item.description:
Overview

This project developed an ankle-based balance strategy that enables a NAO humanoid robot to imitate human motion while preserving stability. The work connects whole-body imitation with balance control so that a humanoid can reproduce a demonstrator’s movement without losing its support constraints.

Technical Approach

The controller uses an inverted-pendulum model based on the robot’s computed center of mass, calculates support polygons for double- and single-support phases, and treats the ground projection of the center of mass as a balance criterion. Ankle-joint corrections are produced with a PID controller whose initial coefficients were estimated with the Ziegler-Nichols method and then tuned for imitation behavior.

Contribution

Simulation and practical tests on a NAO H-25 V4 showed that the approach improves balance during soft-real-time whole-body and quasi-static imitation. The project demonstrates a control-oriented route to more stable, expressive humanoid imitation for human-robot interaction research.

Project Links
